title: Greg Treat's Mind-Numbingly Ignorant Opposition to HB1236
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman dismantles Oklahoma Senate Pro-Tem Greg Treat's six-sentence statement opposing HB1236 by Charles McCall, identifying five separate falsehoods about nullification, the separation of powers, and American history. The article argues Treat's substitute amendment guts the bill of any meaningful nullifying language while pretending to add 'real teeth.'
audiences: history-minded Christian skeptical of states' rights rhetoric
pro-life voter unfamiliar with the abolitionist critique of incrementalism

questions.question: What is HB1236 actually trying to do, and why does Free the States consider it a good bill in need of an amendment?
questions.answer: HB1236 by Charles McCall recognizes Oklahoma's right to review federal actions and declare them unconstitutional. FTS supports the bill but wants it amended to also cover unconstitutional abuses of the federal judiciary, not just Congress and the executive.
questions.quote: Oklahoma House Bill 1236 by Charles McCall is a bill to recognize the right of Oklahoma to review certain actions of the federal government and declare them unconstitutional if appropriate.

questions.question: How does Silberman argue that nullification actually upholds, rather than violates, the separation of powers?
questions.answer: He argues separation of powers exists precisely so that one branch or jurisdiction can check another's unconstitutional acts. Treat's vision of unconditional state submission to federal commands is the opposite of separation of powers; states uphold the doctrine when they reject federal overreach.
questions.quote: The separation of powers was established for the express purpose of empowering government actors to check the unconstitutional usurpations of other government actors. Treat’s idea that one governing entity is unconditionally bound to submission to another entity is the exact opposite of the separation of powers doctrine.

questions.question: What concrete examples does the article cite to show that nullification is not a 'failed legal theory'?
questions.answer: Oklahoma's marijuana legalization effectively nullified the federal Controlled Substances Act and Gonzales v. Raich. The Oklahoma Senate (with Treat's vote) passed SB631 nullifying federal gun regulations, and Treat himself authored SB1379 in 2016 to nullify federal EPA Clean Power Plan rules.
questions.quote: Both the federal Controlled Substances Act and the *Gonzales v. Raich* supreme court decision were nullified in Oklahoma when we legalized the drug. The Senate, Treat included, passed SB631 which nullifies unconstitutional federal gun regulations earlier this session. Treat himself authored SB1379 in 2016 [nullifying federal EPA regulations]

questions.question: Historically, who actually used nullification in pre-Civil War America: the slave states or the abolitionists?
questions.audience: history-minded Christian skeptical of states' rights rhetoric
questions.answer: Nullification was practiced by Northern abolitionists against the Fugitive Slave Act, and the slave states despised it. The article cites the rescue of Joshua Glover in Wisconsin, the Jerry Rescue in Syracuse, and personal liberty laws in Vermont, Michigan, and Massachusetts as examples.
questions.quote: In the years preceding slavery’s abolition, nullification was practiced exclusively by abolitionists to the acute dismay of the slave states. To oppose nullification in the context of American history is to side with the slavers.

questions.question: How did Jefferson Davis himself view nullification, and why does that undercut Treat's claim that nullification was a Confederate tactic?
questions.audience: history-minded Christian skeptical of states' rights rhetoric
questions.answer: Davis explicitly distinguished nullification from secession and complained that Northern legislatures used nullification to thwart the Fugitive Slave Act. Far from championing nullification, Davis hated it because it was being used against slavery.
questions.quote: [State legislatures] made use of the powers and opportunities of the offices held under its sanctions to nullify its obligations and neutralize its guarantees. The halls of Congress afforded the vantage ground from which assaults were made upon these guarantees.

questions.question: What rhetorical move does Silberman use to characterize Treat's claim that nullification 'undermines the tenets of our republic'?
questions.audience: pro-life voter unfamiliar with the abolitionist critique of incrementalism
questions.answer: He calls it one of the most absurd claims ever made about America, then ties it directly to abortion: Treat's posture of unconditional submission has, in practice, kept Oklahoma government complicit in the killing of more than 5,000 babies annually under the guise of respecting 'tenets of the republic.'
questions.quote: Treat has made certain for the past three years that the Oklahoma State government remains the overseer of the murder of more than 5,000 babies annually, claiming adherence to “the tenets of the republic” as he does it.

questions.question: What specific enforcement language did the House version of HB1236 contain that gave the bill its 'teeth'?
questions.answer: The House version forbade the state, counties, political subdivisions, and publicly funded organizations from implementing any federal action determined to be unconstitutional by the Attorney General or a legislative majority. That refusal-to-implement language is the actual mechanism of nullification.
questions.quote: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the state, county, political subdivision or any other publicly funded organization shall not implement any action that restricts a person’s rights or that the Office of the Attorney General or the Legislature by a majority vote determines to be unconstitutional under subsection A.

questions.question: What does Treat's substitute amendment actually do, and why does Silberman call it ten million dollars to do nothing?
questions.answer: Treat's amendment creates a 'State Reserved Powers Protection Unit Fund' inside the AG's office that can only recommend the AG seek federal-court relief, a power the AG already has. It strips out all language preventing implementation of unconstitutional federal actions, replacing nullifying teeth with an expensive, unaccountable bureaucracy.
questions.quote: Everyone already agrees that the attorney general’s duties already includes challenging unconstitutional federal actions in federal court, so this bill is essentially spending 10 million dollars over the next four years to do nothing. Treat’s amendment removes all language specifying how the state would prevent implementation of unconstitutional actions, so he has placed an expensive, unaccountable bureaucracy in the way of the federal action being declared unconstitutional and taken all nullifying teeth out of the bill.

questions.question: How does the article frame Treat's leadership and what action does it call for from Senate Republicans?
questions.answer: Silberman calls Treat a 'bumbling, bootlicking, fabricator' who keeps child sacrifice legal in Oklahoma, and argues the Senate Republican caucus reflects poorly on themselves by following him. He explicitly urges them to remove Treat from his Pro Tem position at their earliest opportunity.
questions.quote: Their submission to a bumbling, bootlicking, fabricator who is keeping child sacrifice legal in Oklahoma reflects poorly on them for choosing him to lead the caucus. They should take action to remove him from his position as Pro Tem at their soonest opportunity to do so.

related.title: FTS Statement on HB1236 and a Suggested Amendment
related.reason: The companion piece referenced in the opening paragraph, laying out FTS's original position on HB1236 that this article builds on.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: The 2019 article whose extended history of abolitionist nullification (Glover, Booth, Vermont, Davis) is quoted at length to refute Treat's slavery framing.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Earlier FTS profile of Treat's record blocking abolition bills, providing context for the 'three years' of complicity Silberman references.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Documents Treat using the same nullification-equals-Confederacy talking point against an earlier abolition bill, showing this is his pattern.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Parallel case of a pro-life politician using the 'nullification is a failed legal theory' falsehood Silberman dismantles here.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Movement reference page on nullification doctrine for readers who want the underlying constitutional argument outside this specific Oklahoma fight.
